# Reproducibility Checklist {.unnumbered}

Run this pass before you circulate a working paper or submit a
replication package. Each item points to the chapter that explains it.
The standard is the one from [Chapter 1](chapters/01-reproducibility.qmd):
a competent stranger, given only your repository, can regenerate every
result.

## Code is versioned and clean {.unnumbered}

- [ ] The project is a Git repository and the analysis code is committed
  (not just sitting in a folder). See
  [Chapter 5](chapters/05-git-beginners.qmd).
- [ ] `git status -s` is clean: no uncommitted changes, no stray files.
- [ ] History is honest: commit messages describe what changed, and no
  commit silently mixes a refactor with a result change. See
  [Chapter 16](chapters/16-ai-age.qmd).

## Nothing forbidden is in the history {.unnumbered}

- [ ] No data, credentials, or licensed material was ever committed, not
  even in an old commit. Check with `git log` and
  `git status --ignored -s`. See [Chapter 6](chapters/06-never-commit.qmd).
- [ ] A `.gitignore` keeps data, secrets, and build noise out.
- [ ] If a secret was ever exposed, it has been rotated, not just
  removed from the latest commit. See
  [Chapter 6](chapters/06-never-commit.qmd).

## Data has a defined home {.unnumbered}

See [Chapter 8](chapters/08-data-environments.qmd).

- [ ] Raw data either rebuilds from a script with a fixed seed, or is
  deposited somewhere citable (Zenodo, Dataverse, openICPSR) and
  referenced, or, if restricted, is described in a Data Availability
  Statement with access terms. See
  [Chapter 15](chapters/15-replication-package.qmd).
- [ ] Derived data are reproducible by-products, not committed artifacts.
- [ ] Random seeds are fixed wherever simulation or sampling occurs.

## The environment is locked {.unnumbered}

See [Chapter 8](chapters/08-data-environments.qmd).

- [ ] A real lockfile is committed (`uv.lock`, `renv.lock`, or a
  `conda-lock` file), not just loose requirements.
- [ ] The result reproduces from a clean clone under the locked
  environment. The running example's invariant is `treat_post = -0.0189`.

## The package is shippable {.unnumbered}

See [Chapter 15](chapters/15-replication-package.qmd).

- [ ] A README states the data source, the software and versions, the
  run order, the expected results, and which output maps to which
  exhibit.
- [ ] A license is present (code and prose may need different ones), and
  a `CITATION.cff` says how to cite the work.
- [ ] The version behind the paper is frozen as a tagged release, and
  (when the work is final) archived to a permanent DOI.

::: {.callout-important .reproducibility title="REPRODUCIBILITY"}
The only test that matters is the clean-clone test: clone the repository
fresh into a new directory, follow your own README touching nothing else,
and confirm the headline result returns. If it does, the package
reproduces. If it does not, fix the package, not your memory of how it
works. See [Chapter 1](chapters/01-reproducibility.qmd).
:::

See the [command cheat sheet](cheatsheet.qmd) for the commands behind
each item.
